title: Making electrocompetent E. coli for plasmid transformation
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"e3ike","text":"\"Quick and Dirty\" way to make E coli strains electrocompetent on a lab bench.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Put the following in the freezer: a) bottle of sterlie diH 2 O (don't let it freeze) b) however many 2mm electro-cuvettes you need
Re-sub 100 µL into 5 mL LB in a universal
Make an Overnight culture of the cells you wish to make competent (24 hours etc)
Grow at 37 °C on shaker for 01:00:00 - 01:15:00 (till about 0.3-0.4 OD 600 )
Once at the right OD, put the cells on ice.
Get two or three boxes of ice
Spin down the 5 mL cells into an eppendorf tube, using a benchtop centrifuge at 14000 x g, °C, . Tip the supernatant into the waste. Keep the cell pellet on ice in between spins.
Once all cells are pelleted, add 1000 µL of ice cold diH 2 O to the tube and resuspend the pellet. Don't worry if the pellet is on the smaller side.
Put the sterile diH 2 O onto ice.
Spin down cells using a benchtop centrifuge at 14000 x g, °C, .
Using a pipette, remove the supernatant smoothly and carefully. Keep an eye on the pellet to make sure that it is not being sucked up in the pipette tip.

Keep adding 1000 µL of ice cold diH 2 O to the tube and resuspending the pellet, spinning down, and removing the supertant with a pipette until the pellet appears "unstable". The more you go through this, your pellet should get paler. Wash, rinse, repeat. Unstable pellet : the pipette tip will start pulling the pipette up with the suction. The best way to determine this is to remove the supernatant by putting the pipette tip close to the pellet and keeping a very careful eye on the cells to make sure they don't get shlurped up.

When the pellet becomes "unstable", add 100 µL of ice cold diH 2 O to the tube. Resuspend the cells. Should be milky white. Keep on ice. Note : if you think you've achieved this with only one wash of cold diH 2 O, you probably haven't. I'd recommend a minimum of two washes/resuspensions before moving on.
Add 40 µL - 50 µL of cells your ice cold electrocuvette. Make sure to place the cells at the bottom of the gap.
Get some SOC media.
Put your cells in their eppendorfs at 37 °C shaking, for anywhere between 00:45:00 and 01:30:00 .
If applicable, do the rest of your electroporations, leaving each cuvette with the SOC media at room temperature.
Remove the cuvette from the machine as soon as possible and immediately add 900 µL of SOC media. Keep on the bench. Do not put back on the ice.

Electroporate one cuvette at a time, carefully wiping down the outside to make sure it's dry. Press the two electroporation buttons simultaneous and wait for the sound of the current to go through. As soon as you hear that sound, you can let go of the buttons. You should see a time constant flash up on the screen. Anything between 4-6 seconds as a time constant should not be a cause for alarm. Note : if you happen to arc your cells (as in, there is a big flash and a bang), you probably didn't get your cell pellet sufficiently clean enough. This happens when there are some salts still in the solution, and your cells are now dead. RIP. If you have more cells left that you have not put in a cuvette, you can take this time to re-wash via ice cold diH 2 O, spinning, so on. If not, you're back to square one. This is a rare occurrence, so don't panic prematurely.
Set the electroporation machine to 2.5 V, 200 U.
Take the cuvette(s), SOC media, 1000 uL pipette, and a sweetie jar for tip disposal to the electroporation station.
Give a gentle tap to the side of the cuvette in order to mix the plasmid and the cells. Return cuvette to ice.

Assuming you have a reasonable amount of DNA in your plasmid sample, you can add between 1 µL - 2 µL of plasmid DNA. If you want to take no chances, 5 µL will probably be more than ample. Again, make sure to place at the bottom of the gap, ideally into the cells. Reasonable DNA amount being somewhere 20+ ng/uL.
Once you have done all of your electroporations, use a pipette to remove all of the liquid from the cuvette and put into a fresh sterile eppendorf.
Plate 100 µL of cells onto the correct selection media plate. Let dry under Bunsen burner.

Spin the remaining volume of cells down on the benchtop centrifuge. Pour off supertant and resuspend in small amount of remaining media. Plate the spun down cells on a selection media plate. This will increase the chance of getting back a transformed colony, if the transformation efficiency seems to be low.
Grow plates Overnight in 37 °C incubator.
creator: Rebecca Quinn
